<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- The presence of elapsed-time-memoization.test.ts without a corresponding production file in this zone suggests its subject (elapsed-time.ts) lives in web-dashboard — the test was pulled here by import proximity to tick-timer, indicating a potential zone misclassification.
- tick-timer.ts is a pure utility with no outbound coupling (coupling: 0.0) and perfect internal cohesion — a well-isolated, testable primitive.
- Four inbound imports from web-viewer confirm tick-timer is a shared polling primitive; if additional timer utilities are added, this zone forms a natural foundation for a polling-primitives module.
- elapsed-time-memoization.test.ts appears to test a component (elapsed-time.ts) that belongs to web-dashboard, not this zone — consider relocating the test to its subject's zone to avoid misleading zone membership.
- tick-timer.ts achieves perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling (0.0), making it a textbook example of a well-isolated utility primitive.
- With 27 incoming call-graph edges and only 1 outgoing, tick-timer has the highest fan-in ratio of any zone in this batch. It functions as a shared polling clock for the entire viewer layer, analogous to a system timer primitive. No other utility zone approaches this level of consumption.
- tick-timer's 27:1 incoming-to-outgoing call ratio makes it the highest fan-in utility in the web package. Any change to its interface propagates to 27 call sites — it warrants a stable, explicitly versioned API contract and should never import from its consumers.
- tick-timer has zero coupling to web-dashboard at the call level (1 outgoing edge, none to web-dashboard) while web-dashboard has 4 inbound import edges from it — the dependency flows strictly upward from primitive to consumer, confirming a clean unidirectional layering that the zone-level bidirectional label obscures.
- tick-timer.ts and crash-detector.ts both implement zero-framework singleton services with module-level mutable state and a `reset*()` function for test isolation. This is an emergent shared pattern without a documented abstraction — if more singleton browser services are added, a shared `BrowserSingleton` lifecycle protocol would reduce the risk of omitting the reset contract.
- [call graph] 123 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 27 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
